title: SEGMENT 4 — Mouse blastoids: Rivron's 2018 proof of concept

lines.text: SEGMENT 4 — Mouse blastoids: Rivron's 2018 proof of concept

In May 2018, Nicolas Rivron, then at MERLN Institute in Maastricht and now at the Institute of Molecular Biotechnology in Vienna, published a paper in Nature that, in retrospect, marks the formal beginning of the synthetic embryo era.
The paper was titled "Blastocyst-like structures generated solely from stem cells," and what it showed was simple, striking, and consequential.
Rivron took mouse embryonic stem cells and mixed them with mouse trophoblast stem cells — both of which, recall, had been derivable as stable lines since the 1980s and 1990s respectively.
He put them together in small microwells, in a defined medium, with a specific signaling cocktail.
The cocktail included a Wnt activator and a TGF-beta inhibitor and a couple of other ingredients, fine-tuned to push the two cell types into the right conversation.
And he watched what happened.
What happened was that the two cell types self-organized.
The trophoblast cells migrated to the outside of the aggregate.
The embryonic stem cells clustered on the inside.
A central cavity opened up, mimicking the blastocoel.
The whole structure, within a couple of days, looked under the microscope like a mouse blastocyst — same size, same general organization, same proportions of cells in each lineage.
The structures even expressed the molecular markers you'd expect: Cdx2 in the outer trophoblast cells, Oct4 and Nanog in the inner cell mass, and so on.
He called them blastoids.
The blastocyst-like structure made from stem cells alone.
Now, the easy version of this experiment would have been to make a structure that just looked like a blastocyst.
Rivron went further.
He asked whether the blastoid could actually do what a blastocyst does.
The defining functional test of a blastocyst is implantation — it has to be able to attach to the wall of a uterus and start the process of forming a placenta.
So Rivron transferred blastoids into the uteruses of pseudopregnant mice.
And in a fraction of cases, the blastoids implanted.
They attached to the uterine wall.
They began to induce decidualization — the maternal tissue response to an implanting embryo.
They started to form the early structures of a developing pregnancy.
They didn't go all the way.
Most of them aborted within a few days, never developing into a recognizable embryo.
The efficiency was low and the developmental fidelity was imperfect.
But the fact that they implanted at all, that they could trigger the maternal response, was the proof of concept.
A structure made from stem cells alone, in a dish, can behave like a real blastocyst — well enough to engage with a uterus.
That's the core demonstration.
Take iPSCs (or in this case mouse ESCs) and trophoblast stem cells, gel them together with the right cocktail, get a structure that looks like and partially behaves like a blastocyst.
The recipe is reproducible.
The yields are decent.
And the resulting structures are, for many practical purposes, models of the real thing.
Rivron's paper opened the floodgates.
The recipe was clear, the principle was demonstrated, and labs around the world started adapting it.

In the next couple of years, multiple groups produced mouse blastoid variants — using different cell sources, different timings, different cocktails — and the field rapidly converged on what worked and what didn't.
A few key technical points are worth noting, because they recur throughout the field.
Microwells matter.
You don't just throw the cells in a dish and hope.
You typically aggregate them in defined micro-cavities — tiny wells just big enough for one blastoid to form.
This standardizes the geometry, gets the cell numbers right, and dramatically improves reproducibility.
Without microwells, you get a mess of variable aggregates.
With them, you get something close to one blastoid per well, all roughly the same size.
Cell ratios matter.
A real blastocyst has, roughly, more trophoblast cells than inner cell mass cells.
The synthetic recipes get the ratio right by starting with a defined number of each cell type per microwell.
Get the ratio wrong, and the structure fails to organize properly.
The signaling cocktail is critical.
The cells, on their own, are not always smart enough to figure out the right organization.

You have to push them with the right signals — Wnt to maintain pluripotency in some cells, TGF-beta inhibition to drive trophoblast identity in others, and various other small molecules to coordinate the timing.
Quality control matters.
Not every aggregate becomes a proper blastoid.
Many fail.
The successful ones look like real blastocysts; the failed ones look like disorganized clumps.
Labs typically score blastoid morphology under the microscope and only count the well-formed ones as "blastoids" for downstream experiments.
Rivron's paper, and the wave of follow-ups it inspired, established the synthetic-embryo field as a legitimate area of research.
It also raised, immediately, the questions that the field has been wrestling with ever since.
If a structure made from stem cells can implant into a uterus and start a pregnancy, even briefly, what is it?
Is it an embryo?
Is it a model of an embryo?
Is it a research tool?
Is it a moral subject?
The mouse work pushed these questions to the surface without yet making them urgent — because mouse work, however striking, doesn't engage human embryo ethics directly.
But everyone in the field knew, in 2018, that the same recipe was going to be tried with human cells the moment human trophoblast stem cells were robustly available.
And human trophoblast stem cells, courtesy of Okae's 2018 paper, became available exactly then.
So the next phase, which we'll get to in Segment 6, was inevitable.
The technical pieces were in place.
The race to make a human blastoid was on.
